summary: Gruber argues that Apple was right to improve MacBook quality and design rather than chase the low-cost netbook trend that analysts and the business press were demanding. He highlights COO Tim Cook's presentation showing that price has never been a main factor in Mac success, pointing instead to better hardware, software, Vista's failures, marketing, and retail stores. Apple's strategy of premium pricing yields 31% of U.S. retail computer revenue from just 18% unit share. Gruber contends that analysts calling for $800 MacBooks fundamentally misunderstand Apple's business. He speculates that if Apple ever enters the netbook market, it would build up from iPhone OS rather than strip down Mac OS X.
key_points: Apple responded to analyst pressure for cheap laptops by doing the opposite: improving build quality with unibody aluminum construction and better graphics
Tim Cook's six reasons for Mac growth deliberately excluded price, listing better computers, software, compatibility, Vista, marketing, and retail stores instead
Apple captures 31% of U.S. retail computer revenue with only 18% unit share, proving the premium strategy works
Other PC makers can only compete on price because they share the same OS, similar hardware, and lack Apple's marketing and retail capabilities
Gruber speculates a hypothetical cheap Apple netbook would run iPhone OS scaled up, not Mac OS X scaled down
money_quotes: Apple doesn't make computers that people have to buy. They make computers that people want to buy.
Instead of making them cheaper, they made them better.
Cheap laptops creak and squeak at the seams. Apple's new MacBooks are cut from solid blocks of aluminum and hardly have any seams.
31 percent of the money on 18 percent of the unit sales. Those numbers are stunning — and they would not get more impressive by selling $800 MacBooks.
Apple builds things up, not down.
These guys calling for $800 Apple notebooks have the company all wrong.
Cook's list isn't marketing bullshit — it's an accurate, succinct description of Apple's computer business.
key_insight: Apple's refusal to compete on price is not stubbornness but strategy — capturing 31% of revenue from 18% of units proves that quality differentiation is far more profitable than chasing market share.
